responses.rag: RAG is important because it combines retrieval with generation and follows best practices for high quality answers.
responses.infra: I usually use scalable cloud infrastructure, monitoring, and clean deployment processes.
responses.automation: Automation is useful to reduce manual work and improve operational efficiency across teams.

responses.rag: I build robust RAG systems with embeddings, vector databases, and best-practice prompt engineering.
responses.infra: I deploy distributed systems with containers, logging, and strong observability patterns.
responses.automation: I create workflow automations that streamline processes and ensure operational excellence.

responses.rag: I have built enterprise RAG systems using scalable retrieval architecture, robust prompt engineering, and production best practices.
responses.infra: I focus on resilient distributed systems, observability, and clean deployment standards so the platform can scale reliably.
responses.automation: I design automation systems that reduce bottlenecks and improve cross-functional efficiency through orchestration.

responses.rag: We improved answer quality through semantic search, chunking strategy, and prompt optimization across multiple use cases.
responses.infra: I worked on cloud-native infrastructure with monitoring, fault tolerance, and scalable services to support growth.
responses.automation: My automations improved business efficiency by reducing manual work and connecting systems in a robust way.

responses.rag: Our first failure was retrieval recall. We had chunking that was too coarse, then reranking added latency. We tuned top-k and changed metadata filters after seeing hallucinations on narrow queries.
responses.infra: A pgvector index rebuild locked writes longer than expected, so we moved the rollout window and added better timeout controls around the migration path.
responses.automation: In n8n the hardest issue was dedup plus retry. A webhook replay created duplicate rows, so I added idempotency keys and queue-aware retry logic.

responses.rag: I used retrieval mostly for internal docs. The tricky part was threshold tuning because low-quality chunks looked relevant but hurt answer precision.
responses.infra: The production bottleneck was container memory pressure during OCR jobs. We split workloads and improved queue handling instead of just scaling blindly.
responses.automation: A workflow broke because of race conditions between API callbacks and DB writes. I fixed it with clearer event ordering and dedup checks.

responses.rag: We had to narrow retrieval because high recall was surfacing stale internal docs. I added filtering and changed chunk overlap after support tickets exposed the issue.
responses.infra: Our API had sporadic timeout spikes during batch ingestion, so I profiled the worker path and separated CPU-heavy parsing from request handling.
responses.automation: A cron-driven sync was double-writing records when retries overlapped, so I added idempotency and a last-processed watermark.

responses.rag: The issue was not retrieval itself but how agent state interacted with retrieval noise. We had to tighten when the system should re-query versus continue with cached context.
responses.infra: A rollout degraded because autoscaling masked a queueing problem instead of fixing it. We traced saturation back to a noisy downstream dependency and changed backpressure handling.
responses.automation: A multi-step approval flow became unreliable because callbacks arrived out of order. We redesigned the state transition rules and added reconciliation jobs.

responses.rag: I improved retrieval and prompt quality, though I do not remember every threshold we used. We had some latency trade-offs.
responses.infra: I worked with containers and monitoring, and there were some timeout issues during heavier workloads.
responses.automation: I used automations and webhooks to reduce manual work, but I was not always the main owner of the workflow logic.

responses.rag: I know the general architecture but my direct work was more on integration than model behavior.
responses.infra: I can explain deployments at a high level, but deeper bottlenecks were usually handled with support from the broader team.
responses.automation: I built working automations, though some of the harder retry and concurrency logic was collaborative.

responses.rag: I worked on retrieval projects and remember tuning some parameters, but deeper ranking decisions were usually led by another engineer.
responses.infra: I supported deployments and incident handling, though I was not the person designing the scaling strategy.
responses.automation: I shipped automations that worked in production, but some concurrency and recovery logic was shared work across the team.

responses.rag: My involvement was lighter on model behavior and heavier on integrating retrieval into the workflow, so I know the system but not every retrieval trade-off.
responses.infra: I handled application deployment and environment setup, but deeper performance issues were usually investigated with senior support.
responses.automation: I owned several workflow builds, although some of the more advanced retry and queue logic came later with help from the team.
